does: This paper introduces an unsupervised framework for adapting protein language models to generate desired proteins without requiring labeled training data or wet-lab validation. The core contribution is identifying task-agnostic proxy rewards that combine model uncertainty with semantic consistency from protein representation models, then proposing two offline reinforcement learning algorithms—Soft Reward Optimization (SRO) and Binarized Reward Optimization (BRO)—that optimize these rewards to steer protein generation. The methods are evaluated on compositional out-of-distribution prompts, where both substantially outperform baselines like DPO and KTO and approach oracle performance across multiple metrics.
connects: This work addresses a central challenge in applying language models to protein design: the supervision bottleneck where controllable generation typically demands expensive wet-lab experiments or hand-curated preference datasets. It builds on recent advances in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and direct preference optimization (DPO) by developing unsupervised alternatives that avoid the need for ground-truth labels. The approach combines insights from uncertainty quantification in neural networks with protein representation learning, extending existing ideas about proxy rewards and offline RL to the protein domain while drawing on broader patterns of self-supervised learning that have proven successful in protein modeling.
enables: This framework opens pathways for scalable, label-free adaptation of protein language models across diverse biological objectives—from improving stability or solubility to engineering novel enzymatic functions—without requiring expensive experimental validation loops. The unsupervised reward design could generalize to other domains where ground-truth supervision is costly, establishing a template for self-directed model steering. Future work can explore hierarchical reward combinations for multi-objective protein design, integration with actual experimental feedback to refine proxy rewards iteratively, and extension to other biomolecular design problems like antibody or RNA engineering where wet-lab bottlenecks similarly constrain progress.
